name: schema
context: Define Prisma data models, datasources, generators, relations, and enums in schema.prisma
info: The Prisma schema file (`prisma/schema.prisma`) is the single source of truth for your database structure. It defines three block types: `datasource` (database connection), `generator` (client output), and `model` (tables/collections). Models declare fields with scalar types (String, Int, Boolean, DateTime, Float, Decimal, BigInt, Bytes, Json), field modifiers (`?` for optional, `[]` for lists), and attributes (`@id`, `@unique`, `@default`, `@relation`, `@map`, `@updatedAt`, `@db.*`). Block-level attributes (`@@id`, `@@unique`, `@@index`, `@@map`, `@@fulltext`) define composite constraints and indexes. Enums define fixed value sets. After editing the schema, run `npx prisma migrate dev` to generate migrations or `npx prisma db push` for prototyping. See [[prisma/migrate]] for migration workflow and [[prisma/client]] for querying.
triggers: add a field to a prisma model
add an index to prisma model
create a composite key in prisma
body:
# Prisma Schema

The schema file at `prisma/schema.prisma` defines your entire data model. Every model, relation, enum, and database connection lives here.

## File Structure

```prisma
// ─── Datasource ─────────────────────────────────
datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"  // postgresql | mysql | sqlite | sqlserver | mongodb | cockroachdb
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}

// ─── Generator ──────────────────────────────────
generator client {
  provider = "prisma-client-js"
}

// ─── Models ─────────────────────────────────────
model User {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  email     String   @unique
  name      String?
  role      Role     @default(USER)
  posts     Post[]
  profile   Profile?
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt
}

model Post {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  title     String
  content   String?
  published Boolean  @default(false)
  author    User     @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
  authorId  Int
  tags      Tag[]
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  @@index([authorId])
  @@index([published, createdAt])
}

model Profile {
  id     Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  bio    String?
  user   User   @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  userId Int    @unique
}

model Tag {
  id    Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  name  String @unique
  posts Post[]
}

// ─── Enums ──────────────────────────────────────
enum Role {
  USER
  ADMIN
  MODERATOR
}
```

## Scalar Types

| Prisma Type | PostgreSQL | MySQL | SQLite |
|-------------|-----------|-------|--------|
| `String` | `text` | `varchar(191)` | `TEXT` |
| `Int` | `integer` | `int` | `INTEGER` |
| `Boolean` | `boolean` | `tinyint(1)` | `INTEGER` |
| `DateTime` | `timestamp(3)` | `datetime(3)` | `NUMERIC` |
| `Float` | `double precision` | `double` | `REAL` |
| `Decimal` | `decimal(65,30)` | `decimal(65,30)` | `NUMERIC` |
| `BigInt` | `bigint` | `bigint` | `INTEGER` |
| `Bytes` | `bytea` | `longblob` | `BLOB` |
| `Json` | `jsonb` | `json` | N/A |

## Field Attributes

| Attribute | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| `@id` | Primary key | `id Int @id` |
| `@unique` | Unique constraint | `email String @unique` |
| `@default(value)` | Default value | `@default(now())`, `@default(autoincrement())`, `@default(uuid())`, `@default(cuid())` |
| `@updatedAt` | Auto-update timestamp | `updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt` |
| `@relation` | Define relation | `@relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])` |
| `@map("name")` | Map to DB column name | `@map("user_name")` |
| `@db.Type` | Native type annotation | `@db.VarChar(255)`, `@db.Text`, `@db.SmallInt` |
| `@ignore` | Exclude from client | Used for unsupported column types |

## Block-Level Attributes

| Attribute | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| `@@id([fields])` | Composite primary key | `@@id([postId, tagId])` |
| `@@unique([fields])` | Composite unique | `@@unique([firstName, lastName])` |
| `@@index([fields])` | Database index | `@@index([authorId, createdAt])` |
| `@@map("name")` | Map to DB table name | `@@map("blog_posts")` |
| `@@fulltext([fields])` | Fulltext index (MySQL) | `@@fulltext([title, content])` |
| `@@ignore` | Exclude entire model | Used for views or unsupported tables |

## Default Value Functions

| Function | Generates |
|----------|----------|
| `autoincrement()` | Auto-incrementing integer |
| `uuid()` | UUID v4 string |
| `cuid()` | Collision-resistant unique ID |
| `now()` | Current timestamp |
| `dbgenerated("expr")` | Database-level expression |

## Relations

See [[prisma/client/relations]] for detailed relation patterns. Quick reference:

```prisma
// One-to-many: User has many Posts
model User {
  id    Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  posts Post[]
}
model Post {
  id       Int  @id @default(autoincrement())
  author   User @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
  authorId Int
}

// One-to-one: User has one Profile
model Profile {
  id     Int  @id @default(autoincrement())
  user   User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  userId Int  @unique  // @unique makes it one-to-one
}

// Many-to-many (implicit): Prisma manages the join table
model Post {
  tags Tag[]
}
model Tag {
  posts Post[]
}
```

## Referential Actions

Set on `@relation` with `onDelete` and `onUpdate`:

| Action | Behavior |
|--------|----------|
| `Cascade` | Delete/update dependents automatically |
| `Restrict` | Prevent if dependents exist |
| `NoAction` | Like Restrict, checked after all commands |
| `SetNull` | Set FK to null (field must be optional) |
| `SetDefault` | Set FK to its default value |

```prisma
model Post {
  author   User @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  authorId Int
}
```

## CLI Commands

```bash
npx prisma validate    # Check schema syntax
npx prisma format      # Auto-format schema file
npx prisma format --check  # CI check (exit 1 if unformatted)
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|--------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | Database connection string used in `datasource.url` |

Use `env("DATABASE_URL")` in the schema to reference it:
```prisma
datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}
```

sections.title: Datasource configuration
sections.body: The `datasource` block sets the database provider and connection URL. Only one datasource block is allowed. Use `env("DATABASE_URL")` to read the connection string from environment variables. Supported providers: `postgresql`, `mysql`, `sqlite`, `sqlserver`, `mongodb`, `cockroachdb`.

sections.title: Generator configuration
sections.body: The `generator` block configures what client to generate. The standard generator is `prisma-client-js`. You can set `output` to customize the output directory. Run `npx prisma generate` after schema changes to regenerate the client. Multiple generators are supported (e.g., for generating a DBML diagram).

sections.title: Model definitions
sections.body: Models map to database tables (or MongoDB collections). Each model must have at least one field marked `@id` or a `@@id` composite key. Fields have a name, type, optional modifier (`?` for nullable, `[]` for list), and attributes. Use `@map` and `@@map` to customize the underlying database column and table names without changing the Prisma API surface.

sections.title: Enum types
sections.body: Enums define a fixed set of allowed values for a field. Declare with `enum Name { VALUE1 VALUE2 }`. Enums map to native database enums on PostgreSQL and MySQL, and to string checks on SQLite. Use enums for role types, status fields, and any constrained value set.

sections.title: Indexes and constraints
sections.body: Add `@@index([field1, field2])` for composite indexes to speed up queries. Use `@@unique([field1, field2])` for composite unique constraints. For fulltext search on MySQL, use `@@fulltext([field1, field2])`. Place indexes on fields used in `where`, `orderBy`, and relation foreign keys for best query performance.

links.context: Apply schema changes to the database via migrations
Query the database using the generated client

related.note: After editing the schema, run migrate dev to apply changes
The schema defines the types available in the generated client
Relation fields in the schema determine include/connect behavior in queries
